transcript: This is the latest piece I'm working on at the moment it's called a pangolin and as you can see it's very scaly. The scales begin at the tip of the tail that would be the first one that's laid and then it's like tiling a roof all the way up so I can't go back once it's done. This is a very rough underneath layer which allows me to to put the scales on. It doesn't have to be specially well made. I have to finish the feet first before I can lay the scales along the sides so I'll be working my way up the legs next and putting the pieces of skin which have also been textured over the claws. The scales are made by cutting out a blank like this and polishing the edges. It's annealed which is softening it and here I've got a very simple block of wood with a sculpted depression in it to hammer the scales into so they have a nice curvature for putting on the curved body of the animal. When the scales are finished right up to the neck I then decide what size the head should be to be in scale with the bulk of the body and then the last thing that I have to do is to polish I don't know what you're talking about. I don't know what you're talking about. I don't know what you're talking about. I don't know what you're talking about. I don't know what you're talking about. decide what size the head should be to be in scale with the bulk of the body and then the last thing that I have to do is to polish the whole animal up like a shiny new penny very like that and then put a large flame on it quite hot and color it and as you can see little bits of color are showing on this underlying layer and I will try to get a nice deep sort of brown colour to colour the animal.
